_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Shelby County, IN. Record survival for the county centered on Shelbyville. Well-preserved from 1822 organization.

previous_courthouses.fate: Brick courthouse. Served through the Civil War and industrial era. Replaced by Art Deco current building.

record_losses.description: Shelby County's records are well-preserved from the 1822 organization. No major fire, flood, or war damage. Records run continuously.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in Shelby County, the records survive. For Black families in the Bear Creek settlement, county deed records document land ownership that was rare for African Americans in antebellum Indiana.
record_losses.source: Indiana Commission on Public Records. Guide to Indiana County Records.

deeds.repository: Shelby County Recorder; Indiana State Archives

wills_estates.repository: Shelby County Clerk; Indiana State Archives

marriages.repository: Shelby County Clerk; Indiana State Archives

substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for tracking Bear Creek free Black families. The 1850 and 1860 census schedules name these families and document their farms.

substitute_sources.description: Methodist, Baptist, and Christian Church records serve most of Shelby County. Some Quaker records from eastern Shelby County.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual congregations; Indiana United Methodist Archives at DePauw University
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Supplement county records before vital registration.

narrative_hooks: Shelby County's records survive complete from 1822 — {ancestor_name}'s property records, marriages, and court appearances are at Shelbyville.
For Bear Creek settlement families, the deed records at Shelbyville document Black land ownership in antebellum Indiana — rare and genealogically powerful evidence.
Before 1882, a missing vital record for {ancestor_name} is standard across Indiana — church records and census are the primary substitutes.
The 1936 Art Deco courthouse at Shelbyville replaced the building where {ancestor_name}'s grandfather's will was probably filed — the records transferred even if the building changed.
source_references: Indiana Commission on Public Records. Guide to Indiana County Records.
Chadwick, Edward H. History of Shelby County, Indiana. Indianapolis: B.F. Bowen, 1909.
Indiana United Methodist Archives, DePauw University.
